segments.title: How the London Naval Treaty defined little ships
segments.summary: The 1930 London Naval Treaty shows how tonnage, speed, guns, and torpedo equipment separated exempt small vessels from destroyers and cruisers.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke works through Articles 8 and 15 of the 1930 London Naval Treaty, explaining the tonnage and armament tests applied to small warships. The exemptions left room for Britain to build numerous ocean-going escorts equipped for minesweeping and anti-submarine work, while the cruiser rules encouraged destroyer guns below the 5.1-inch threshold. The discussion makes treaty categories look less like neutral labels and more like negotiated rules that favored particular naval requirements.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Articles 8 and 15, their tonnage and weapon limits, and the Royal Navy capabilities left outside aggregate tonnage.

segments.title: Abdiel-class cruisers beat distance with speed and mines
segments.summary: The Abdiel class used 40-knot speed to reach distant passages, lay mines before heavy opposition arrived, and withdraw quickly.
segments.body: The Abdiel-class ships sat awkwardly in treaty categories: at roughly 2,650 tons and armed with six 4-inch guns, they counted as unusually small light cruisers rather than exempt little ships. Their purpose explains the design. High speed and useful range let them answer the tyranny of distance by racing to a port or sea lane, laying a blocking minefield, and escaping before stronger forces could intervene.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke classifies the Abdiel class and describes its fast offensive minelaying role.

segments.title: HMS Bassett as an ocean-going trawler prototype
segments.summary: HMS Bassett gave the Royal Navy and its partners a compact prewar model for armed trawlers, minesweeping, and anti-submarine duties.
segments.body: Ordered under the Henderson-Goodall program, HMS Bassett provided a working model of an ocean-going vessel that remained below the treaty's 600-ton threshold. Her modest speed and armament were paired with minesweeping equipment and depth charges, making the design adaptable for British, Canadian, and Indian service. Her survival of a 1942 air attack also illustrates the value of robust construction in a small auxiliary warship.

evidence.note: The transcript gives Bassett's origins, dimensions, roles, armament, and reported resistance to air attack.

segments.title: Admirable-class minesweepers packed escort weapons into a small hull
segments.summary: The Admirable class combined minesweeping gear, anti-aircraft guns, Hedgehog, and depth charges at the cost of a remarkably crowded crew.
segments.body: At about 625 tons, the Admirable class fell just beyond the treaty's smallest exemption but remained within its broader slow-vessel category. Dr. Clarke contrasts its dimensions with HMS Bassett and questions how a complement of roughly 104 could fit aboard. The answer lies partly in a much denser weapon and sensor workload, including 3-inch and 40 mm guns, 20 mm weapons, Hedgehog, depth-charge gear, and two minesweeping arrangements.

evidence.note: The transcript compares Admirable-class size and complement with Bassett and lists its extensive armament and minesweeping equipment.

segments.title: Italy's RD-class minesweepers carried an old fleet through another war
segments.summary: The small RD class formed the Regia Marina's most coherent minesweeping group despite interwar age and inconsistent light armament.
segments.body: The roughly 207-ton RD boats were small, lightly crewed, and fitted with whatever armament was available, making exact weapon fits difficult to generalize. Built from 1916 into the 1920s, they remained numerous enough to form the Regia Marina's most homogeneous minesweeping force in the Second World War. Their continued importance shows how specialist auxiliaries can remain operationally essential long after their designs cease to look modern.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke describes RD-class dimensions, uncertain armament, construction history, surviving numbers, and wartime minesweeping importance.

segments.title: Vosper 73-foot boats and the Admiralty fight over coastal strike craft
segments.summary: The Vosper Type I and II pair compact torpedo armament with a procurement struggle over whether small attack craft threatened capital-ship budgets.
segments.body: Britain produced 29 Vosper 73-foot Type I and II boats, but their acquisition became entangled in Admiralty politics. Supporters associated with Reginald Henderson and Stanley Goodall saw value in a treaty-exempt coastal striking force, while opponents feared competition with battleships and carriers. The Type I carried four 18-inch torpedoes; the preferred Type II traded two torpedoes for a quick-firing 6-pounder, shifting the design toward heavier gun power.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Vosper production, Admiralty opposition, dimensions, nominal speed, and the armament difference between Types I and II.

segments.title: Elco 80-foot PT boats worked best as ambush predators
segments.summary: PT-109 illustrates how speed, torpedoes, and crew-added weapons made Elco boats dangerous in ambush but vulnerable in a fleet action.
segments.body: The 326 Elco 80-foot boats collectively represented a large torpedo inventory, but Dr. Clarke warns against imagining them massed in an idealized charge against a prepared fleet. Radar, gunfire, coordination, and logistics would make that scenario unrealistic. Their real strength was as small, fast ambush craft, exemplified by PT-109's four 21-inch torpedoes and a gun battery that crews often augmented in service.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke uses PT-109 and the Elco production total to contrast unrealistic mass attacks with the boats' effective ambush role.

segments.title: Schnellboots brought greater size and firepower to the torpedo-boat fight
segments.summary: The German Schnellboot combined high speed, reload torpedoes, and multiple cannon in a hull roughly twice the displacement of a Vosper boat.
segments.body: Allied E-boats, or German Schnellboots, displaced roughly 70 to 100 tons and could make about 43.8 knots. They carried two torpedoes ready in their tubes, two reloads, several 20 mm cannon, and a 37 mm weapon. Comparing them with the much lighter Vosper design explains why the Royal Navy developed gun-heavy craft specifically able to hunt them.

evidence.note: The transcript compares Schnellboot displacement, speed, crew, torpedoes, and cannon with the smaller Vosper boat.

segments.title: Fairmile D dog boats hunted Schnellboots with guns and depth charges
segments.summary: The heavily armed Fairmile D answered the E-boat threat and even used depth-charge blasts to disrupt fast surface targets.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke classifies the Fairmile D dog boat by function: although labels varied, its heavy guns and frequent employment against torpedo boats make it a motor gunboat. Its battery included 6-pounders, 20 mm Oerlikons, Vickers machine guns, and depth charges. Crews could drop depth charges ahead of a pursuing Schnellboot so the underwater blast disrupted or disabled it, leaving the German boat exposed to gunfire.

evidence.note: The transcript defines the Fairmile D by its gunboat role, lists its weapons, and explains the depth-charge tactic against Schnellboots.

segments.title: Elco S-class gunboats traded torpedoes for concentrated automatic fire
segments.summary: John F. Kennedy's later Elco command shows a Pacific gunboat layout built around machine guns and 40 mm cannon rather than torpedoes.
segments.body: The Elco S-class PTB gunboat displaced about 41 tons and retained a nominal 41-knot speed, but its purpose was expressed through guns rather than torpedoes. Dr. Clarke describes a dense battery of .50-caliber machine guns, 40 mm cannon, and lighter weapons. He contrasts this Pacific configuration with British Channel gunboats, showing how different opponents and operating areas produced different small-craft solutions.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Kennedy's second command and details the Elco S-class gunboat's dimensions, speed, and concentrated gun armament.

segments.title: Spica-class torpedo boats sustained Italy's convoy war
segments.summary: The Spica class combined torpedoes, mines, and strong gun armament in an escort that the Regia Marina depended on for Mediterranean convoys.
segments.body: Although officially torpedo boats, the thousand-ton Spica class carried three 3.9-inch guns, numerous light anti-aircraft weapons, four 18-inch torpedoes, and up to twenty mines. Dr. Clarke therefore treats them as torpedo gunboats whose gun battery made them effective against smaller coastal craft. They were outmatched by full destroyers, but their escort work was indispensable to the Regia Marina's ability to operate and protect convoys in the Mediterranean.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke explains his torpedo-gunboat classification and the Spica class's armament, convoy importance, and combat relationship with destroyers.
